schools.summary: Moses speaking his final words to all Israel: 'and Moses went and spake these words unto all Israel' — Moses came before the whole assembly to deliver his closing charge (the and Moses went, Moses coming forth, the and spake these words unto all Israel, the final address to the people, the figure, the aged leader's last words to the people he had led, the solemn close of his ministry, the truth that Moses, knowing his end near, gathered the people for his final charge, the figure of the faithful servant finishing his course, the last words to the flock, the parting address of the man of God, the people gathered, the solemn farewell that opens the closing scenes, vv.2); Moses coming before the people (the address) — the coming (Moses went); 'spake these words unto all Israel' (the final charge to the whole people, the parting words, the figure, the faithful servant's last address, the providence, the address, Moses's age and the LORD going over to follow, vv.2-3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Moses went and spake these words unto all Israel
Moses speaking his final words to all Israel

schools.summary: Moses's great age and that he might not cross Jordan: 'and he said unto them, I am an hundred and twenty years old this day; I can no more go out and come in: also the LORD hath said unto me, Thou shalt not go over this Jordan' — Moses, aged 120, told them he could lead them no longer and might not cross Jordan (the I am an hundred and twenty years old this day, the great age, the I can no more go out and come in, no longer able to lead, the also the LORD hath said unto me, Thou shalt not go over this Jordan, barred from the land, the figure, the aged Moses owning his limits and his exclusion, the leadership passing, the truth that Moses, full of years, must give place, barred from the land, the figure of the servant who brings the people to the border but enters not, the law not bringing in, the meekness that owns the bar and the passing of leadership, the close of Moses's leading, the LORD going over instead, vv.3); Moses aged 120, no longer able to lead (the limit) — the age (a hundred and twenty); 'the LORD hath said unto me, Thou shalt not go over this Jordan' (Moses barred from the land, the law not entering, the figure, the servant who enters not the rest, the providence, the exclusion, the LORD going over before them to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he said unto them, I am an hundred and twenty years old this day; I can no more go out and come in: also the LORD hath said unto me, Thou shalt not go over this Jordan
Moses's great age and that he might not cross Jordan
Moses, full of years, owning that he could lead no longer and was barred from the land

schools.summary: That God would go over before them, with Joshua as leader: 'the LORD thy God, he will go over before thee, and he will destroy these nations from before thee, and thou shalt possess them: and Joshua, he shall go over before thee, as the LORD hath said' — God himself would go before them to conquer, with Joshua as the appointed leader (the the LORD thy God, he will go over before thee, God going before, the and he will destroy these nations from before thee, and thou shalt possess them, the conquest his work, the and Joshua, he shall go over before thee, as the LORD hath said, Joshua the leader, the figure, God himself the leader of the conquest, Joshua his appointed servant, the truth that the victory would be God's, Joshua leading under him, the figure of the divine leadership that does not fail when the human leader passes, 'Joshua' (the same name as Jesus) leading the people in, the assurance that God goes before, the conquest secured, the leadership passing to Joshua under God, vv.7); God going before to destroy the nations (the assurance) — the assurance (God before them); 'Joshua, he shall go over before thee, as the LORD hath said' (Joshua the appointed leader under God, the leadership passing, the figure, the divine leadership that does not fail, the providence, Joshua, the encouragement not to fear to follow, vv.4-6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The LORD thy God, he will go over before thee, and he will destroy these nations from before thee, and thou shalt possess them: and Joshua, he shall go over before thee, as the LORD hath said
that God would go over before them, with Joshua as leader
God himself going before to conquer the nations, with Joshua his appointed servant leading the people in
the divine leadership that does not fail when Moses passes

schools.summary: That God would do to the nations as to Sihon and Og: 'and the LORD shall do unto them as he did to Sihon and to Og, kings of the Amorites, and unto the land of them, whom he destroyed' — God would destroy the nations of Canaan as he had destroyed Sihon and Og (the and the LORD shall do unto them as he did to Sihon and to Og, the like judgment, the kings of the Amorites, and unto the land of them, whom he destroyed, the conquered kings, the figure, the recent victory over Sihon and Og the pledge of victory over Canaan, the truth that God who destroyed the two kings would destroy the nations, the past a token of the future, the figure of the encouragement drawn from past deliverances, the pledge of new victories, the conquered kings the proof of God's power, the same to come, the assurance grounded on what God had already done, Numbers 21:24, 34); God doing to the nations as to Sihon and Og (the pledge, Numbers 21:24) — the example (Sihon and Og); 'and unto the land of them, whom he destroyed' (the kings destroyed, the pledge of victory, the past a token, the figure, the encouragement from past deliverances, the providence, the example, the charge to be strong to follow, vv.5-6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD shall do unto them as he did to Sihon and to Og, kings of the Amorites, and unto the land of them, whom he destroyed
that God would do to the nations as to Sihon and Og
God who destroyed the two kings would destroy the nations of Canaan
the recent victory the pledge of victories to come (Numbers 21:24)

schools.summary: That God would give the nations up to be dealt with as commanded: 'and the LORD shall give them up before your face, that ye may do unto them according unto all the commandments which I have commanded you' — God would deliver the nations into their hand, to be dealt with as he had commanded (the and the LORD shall give them up before your face, the nations delivered, the that ye may do unto them according unto all the commandments which I have commanded you, dealt with as commanded, the figure, the nations given up by God, to be dealt with by his command, not their own will, the truth that the conquest was God's gift, and the dealing with the nations was to follow his command, the figure of the obedience that executes God's judgment, the nations given up, the conquest by God's gift and command, the people his instrument, the deliverance that calls for obedience, vv.6); the nations delivered into their hand (the gift) — the deliverance (given up); 'that ye may do unto them according unto all the commandments which I have commanded you' (the nations dealt with by God's command, the obedience, the figure, the people God's instrument, the providence, the deliverance, the charge to be strong to follow, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD shall give them up before your face, that ye may do unto them according unto all the commandments which I have commanded you

the people his instrument in executing his judgment

schools.summary: The charge to be strong and not to fear, for God will never fail nor forsake them: 'be strong and of a good courage, fear not, nor be afraid of them: for the LORD thy God, he it is that doth go with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee' — the unfailing presence — 'be strong and of a good courage, fear not, nor be afraid of them: for the LORD thy God, he it is that doth go with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee': the people charged to be strong and unafraid, because God goes with them and will never abandon them (the witnesses noting the significance — the courage of faith grounded on God's abiding and unfailing presence, the charge 'be strong, fear not' resting on the promise 'he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee,' the ground of all courage, the lesson — that the believer's strength is not in himself but in the unfailing presence of God, the fear of the foe overcome by the certainty that God goes with his people and never forsakes them, the truth that this promise is the believer's stay in every trial, taken up in the New Testament as the ground of contentment and courage, 'he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee. So that we may boldly say, The Lord is my helper,' Hebrews 13:5-6, the figure — the presence of God that is the strength and stay of his people, 'fear thou not; for I am with thee,' Isaiah 41:10, 'lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world,' Matthew 28:20, the truth that God never forsakes his own, the same promise made to Joshua and to every believer, the figure of the courage of faith that rests on the faithfulness of God, the call to be strong because God is faithful, the fear of man swallowed up in the presence of God, the warning against the fear that forgets God's presence, the figure of the courage of faith grounded on God's unfailing presence); the charge to be strong and unafraid before the foe, the courage commanded (the strength of faith, Isaiah 41:10) — the charge (be strong, fear not); 'for the LORD thy God, he it is that doth go with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee' (the ground of courage, God's abiding presence that will never fail nor forsake, the believer's stay in every trial, the unfailing God, Hebrews 13:5-6; Matthew 28:20) — the figure (the presence of God the strength of his people, the providence, the charge, Joshua charged before all Israel to follow, vv.7-8) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What grounds the charge 'be strong and of a good courage, fear not'?

sides.position: God's abiding and unfailing presence - 'he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee'; the believer's strength is not in himself but in the certainty that God goes with his people and never abandons them (Isaiah 41:10), the very promise taken up as the believer's stay against fear and covetousness (Hebrews 13:5-6) and as Christ's parting word, 'lo, I am with you alway' (Matthew 28:20)

themes: Be strong and of a good courage, fear not, nor be afraid of them: for the LORD thy God, he it is that doth go with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee
the charge to be strong and not to fear, for God will never fail nor forsake them (the crux of the courage of faith grounded on God's unfailing presence)
the courage grounded not on human strength but on God's abiding and unfailing presence, the fear of the foe overcome by the certainty that God never abandons his own
the promise taken up as the believer's stay against fear (Isaiah 41:10; Hebrews 13:5-6; Matthew 28:20)

schools.summary: Moses charging Joshua before all Israel to be strong: 'and Moses called unto Joshua, and said unto him in the sight of all Israel, Be strong and of a good courage: for thou must go with this people unto the land which the LORD hath sworn unto their fathers to give them; and thou shalt cause them to inherit it' — Moses publicly charged Joshua to be strong and lead the people into the land (the and Moses called unto Joshua, and said unto him in the sight of all Israel, the public charge, the Be strong and of a good courage, the courage commanded, the for thou must go with this people unto the land which the LORD hath sworn unto their fathers, the task of leading them in, the and thou shalt cause them to inherit it, the inheritance secured, the figure, the public commissioning of Joshua, the charge to courage and the task of leading the people in, the truth that Joshua was openly charged before all, that the people own his leadership, the figure of the appointed successor strengthened for his task, the leader commissioned, the courage commanded for the work of bringing the people into the inheritance, the passing of leadership confirmed before all, vv.8); Joshua charged before all to be strong (the commissioning) — the charge (be strong); 'thou must go with this people unto the land... and thou shalt cause them to inherit it' (Joshua to lead the people into the inheritance, the task, the figure, the appointed successor commissioned, the providence, the charge, the LORD going before Joshua to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Moses called unto Joshua, and said unto him in the sight of all Israel, Be strong and of a good courage: for thou must go with this people unto the land which the LORD hath sworn unto their fathers to give them; and thou shalt cause them to inherit it
Moses charging Joshua before all Israel to be strong
Moses publicly charging Joshua to be strong and lead the people into the land
the appointed successor commissioned for his task before all

schools.summary: The assurance to Joshua that God would go before him and not fail him: 'and the LORD, he it is that doth go before thee; he will be with thee, he will not fail thee, neither forsake thee: fear not, neither be dismayed' — Joshua was assured that God would go before him, be with him, and never fail him (the and the LORD, he it is that doth go before thee, God going before, the he will be with thee, he will not fail thee, neither forsake thee, the unfailing presence, the fear not, neither be dismayed, the charge against fear, the figure, the same promise of God's unfailing presence given to Joshua, the ground of his courage, the truth that Joshua, like the people, was to be strong because God went before and would not forsake him, the figure of the divine presence that strengthens the leader, the same promise repeated, 'I will be with thee,' Joshua 1:5, the assurance that God never fails his servants, the leader upheld, the courage grounded on God's presence, vv.6); God going before Joshua and never failing him (the unfailing presence, Joshua 1:5) — the presence (God before him); 'fear not, neither be dismayed' (the charge against fear, the courage grounded on God's presence, the figure, the divine presence that strengthens, the providence, the assurance, the writing of the law to follow, vv.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD, he it is that doth go before thee; he will be with thee, he will not fail thee, neither forsake thee: fear not, neither be dismayed
the assurance to Joshua that God would go before him and not fail him
Joshua assured that God would go before him, be with him, and never fail him
the same promise of unfailing presence that grounded the people's courage (Joshua 1:5)

schools.summary: That Moses wrote the law and delivered it to the priests and elders: 'and Moses wrote this law, and delivered it unto the priests the sons of Levi, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and unto all the elders of Israel' — the written law — 'and Moses wrote this law, and delivered it unto the priests the sons of Levi, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and unto all the elders of Israel': Moses committed the law to writing and entrusted it to the priests and elders (the witnesses noting the significance — the inscripturation of God's word and its committal to the keeping of his appointed ministers, the law written down by Moses, no longer only spoken, and delivered to the priests and elders to keep and teach, the lesson — that God's word is given in writing, a permanent and public deposit, entrusted to the church to guard and transmit, the written law the abiding rule, committed to those who bore the ark and led the people, the truth that the oracles of God were committed to his people to keep, 'unto them were committed the oracles of God,' Romans 3:2, the figure — the written word of God, the Scripture that abides, 'the scripture cannot be broken,' John 10:35, the truth that the law was written that it might be kept whole, read, and taught to the generations, the deposit of truth guarded by the church, the figure of the committal of the sacred writings to the ministry, 'keep that which is committed to thy trust,' 1 Timothy 6:20, the truth that the written word is the foundation of the public reading and teaching that follows, vv.11, the figure of the church as the keeper and witness of the written word, the dignity and permanence of the inscripturated law, the foundation of all that follows, the figure of the written word of God committed to the keeping of his church); Moses committing the law to writing, the word now inscripturated and permanent (the written law, John 10:35) — the writing (Moses wrote); 'delivered it unto the priests the sons of Levi, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and unto all the elders of Israel' (the law entrusted to the priests and elders to keep and teach, the oracles committed to the church, the deposit guarded, Romans 3:2; 1 Timothy 6:20) — the figure (the church the keeper of the written word, the providence, the law, the command to read it every seven years to follow, vv.10-13) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses writing the law and delivering it to the priests and elders establish?

sides.position: The written word of God committed to the keeping of his church - the inscripturation of God's word as a permanent and public deposit, entrusted to the priests and elders to guard, read, and transmit, the oracles of God committed to his people (Romans 3:2), the Scripture that abides and cannot be broken (John 10:35), the sacred trust to be kept (1 Timothy 6:20)

themes: And Moses wrote this law, and delivered it unto the priests the sons of Levi, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and unto all the elders of Israel
that Moses wrote the law and delivered it to the priests and elders (the crux of the written word of God committed to the keeping of his church)
the inscripturation of God's word as a permanent and public deposit, committed to the priests and elders, the church entrusted to guard and transmit the oracles of God
the Scripture that abides and cannot be broken (Romans 3:2; John 10:35; 1 Timothy 6:20)

schools.summary: The command to read the law at the year of release: 'and Moses commanded them, saying, At the end of every seven years, in the solemnity of the year of release, in the feast of tabernacles' — the law was to be read publicly every seventh year, at the feast of tabernacles (the and Moses commanded them, saying, At the end of every seven years, every seventh year, the in the solemnity of the year of release, the sabbatical year, the in the feast of tabernacles, at the feast, the figure, the appointed time for the public reading of the law, the year of release and the feast, the truth that the law was to be read at the great gathering, that all might hear, the figure of the regular public reading of God's word, the appointed times, the year of release and the feast the fitting occasion, the people assembled, the public reading commanded, vv.11); the seventh year appointed for the reading (the time) — the time (every seven years); 'in the solemnity of the year of release, in the feast of tabernacles' (the sabbatical year and the feast the occasion, the great gathering, the figure, the regular public reading, the providence, the time, the gathering of all the people to follow, vv.11-12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Moses commanded them, saying, At the end of every seven years, in the solemnity of the year of release, in the feast of tabernacles
the command to read the law at the year of release
the law to be read publicly every seventh year at the feast of tabernacles
the great gathering the fitting occasion

schools.summary: The law read before all Israel at the chosen place: 'when all Israel is come to appear before the LORD thy God in the place which he shall choose, thou shalt read this law before all Israel in their hearing' — when the people gathered at the chosen place, the law was to be read in their hearing (the when all Israel is come to appear before the LORD thy God in the place which he shall choose, the gathering at the sanctuary, the thou shalt read this law before all Israel in their hearing, the public reading, the figure, the law read aloud to the assembled people, that all might hear, the truth that the word of God was to be read publicly, the whole nation hearing, the figure of the public reading of Scripture in the congregation, the word heard by all, 'till I come, give attendance to reading,' 1 Timothy 4:13, the people gathered to hear the law, the word proclaimed, the reading that instructs the whole nation, vv.12); the people gathered at the chosen place (the assembly) — the gathering (before the LORD); 'thou shalt read this law before all Israel in their hearing' (the law read in the hearing of all, the public reading, 1 Timothy 4:13, the figure, the public reading of Scripture in the congregation, the providence, the reading, the gathering of men, women, and children to follow, vv.12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When all Israel is come to appear before the LORD thy God in the place which he shall choose, thou shalt read this law before all Israel in their hearing
the law read before all Israel at the chosen place
the law read aloud to the assembled people at the sanctuary, the whole nation hearing the word of God
the public reading that instructs all (1 Timothy 4:13)

schools.summary: The command to gather all the people to hear the law, that they may learn and fear God: 'gather the people together, men, and women, and children, and thy stranger that is within thy gates, that they may hear, and that they may learn, and fear the LORD your God, and observe to do all the words of this law' — the universal instruction — 'gather the people together, men, and women, and children, and thy stranger that is within thy gates, that they may hear, and that they may learn, and fear the LORD your God, and observe to do all the words of this law': all the people, even women, children, and strangers, were to be gathered to hear the law (the witnesses noting the significance — the public reading of the word for the instruction of the whole people, none excluded, men, women, children, and strangers all gathered, that all might hear and learn, the lesson — that God's word is for all his people, the youngest and the stranger included, that they learn to fear God and obey, the end of the hearing is to learn, to fear, and to do, not mere hearing but instruction unto obedience, the truth that all are to hear and learn the word of God, 'from a child thou hast known the holy scriptures,' 2 Timothy 3:15, the figure — the universal call to hear and learn God's word, 'faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God,' Romans 10:17, the truth that the public reading and teaching of Scripture is the means by which the people, young and old, learn to fear God and obey, the figure of the church gathered to hear the word, the children and strangers not excluded from the means of grace, the truth that the end of hearing is learning, fearing, and doing, the word bearing fruit in reverence and obedience, the call to bring all, even the little ones, under the teaching of God's word, the figure of the public reading of God's word for the instruction of the whole people unto fear and obedience); all the people gathered, men, women, children, and strangers, none excluded from hearing (the universal instruction, 2 Timothy 3:15) — the gathering (all the people); 'that they may hear, and that they may learn, and fear the LORD your God, and observe to do all the words of this law' (the end of the hearing, that they learn, fear God, and obey, the word unto reverence and obedience, the instruction unto obedience, Romans 10:17) — the figure (the church gathered to hear the word, the providence, the command, the children to hear that they may learn to follow, vv.13) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the command to gather all to hear the law establish?

sides.position: The public reading of God's word for the instruction of the whole people unto fear and obedience - all gathered, men, women, children, and strangers, none excluded, that they hear, learn, fear God, and obey, the youngest and the stranger included in the means of grace (2 Timothy 3:15), faith and reverence coming by the hearing of the word (Romans 10:17), the end of hearing being learning, fearing, and doing

themes: Gather the people together, men, and women, and children, and thy stranger that is within thy gates, that they may hear, and that they may learn, and fear the LORD your God, and observe to do all the words of this law
the command to gather all the people to hear the law, that they may learn and fear God (the crux of the public reading of God's word for the instruction of the whole people)
the word for the instruction of the whole people, none excluded, that all might hear, learn, fear God, and obey, the youngest and the stranger included
faith and reverence coming by the hearing of the word, the end of hearing being learning, fearing, and doing (2 Timothy 3:15; Romans 10:17)

schools.summary: That the children too should hear and learn to fear God: 'and that their children, which have not known any thing, may hear, and learn to fear the LORD your God, as long as ye live in the land whither ye go over Jordan to possess it' — even the children who knew nothing were to hear and learn the fear of God (the and that their children, which have not known any thing, the ignorant children, the may hear, and learn to fear the LORD your God, that they learn the fear of God, the as long as ye live in the land whither ye go over Jordan to possess it, all their days in the land, the figure, the children, ignorant as yet, to be brought under the word that they learn to fear God, the truth that the rising generation was to be instructed in the fear of God from the word, the figure of the teaching of the children, the word handed down to those who knew nothing, 'train up a child in the way he should go,' Proverbs 22:6, the fear of God learned by the children through the public reading, the instruction of the next generation, vv.12); the ignorant children brought to hear and learn the fear of God (the instruction, Proverbs 22:6) — the children (taught); 'as long as ye live in the land' (the instruction continuing all their days, the perpetual teaching, the figure, the word handed down to the children, the providence, the children, the LORD calling Moses and Joshua to the tabernacle to follow, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And that their children, which have not known any thing, may hear, and learn to fear the LORD your God, as long as ye live in the land whither ye go over Jordan to possess it
that the children too should hear and learn to fear God
even the children who knew nothing brought under the word that they learn the fear of God
the rising generation instructed all their days in the land (Proverbs 22:6)

schools.summary: God calling Moses and Joshua to the tabernacle to charge Joshua: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Behold, thy days approach that thou must die: call Joshua, and present yourselves in the tabernacle of the congregation, that I may give him a charge. And Moses and Joshua went, and presented themselves in the tabernacle of the congregation' — God told Moses his death was near and called him and Joshua to the tabernacle for Joshua's charge (the and the LORD said unto Moses, Behold, thy days approach that thou must die, Moses's death near, the call Joshua, and present yourselves in the tabernacle of the congregation, that I may give him a charge, Joshua to be charged, the and Moses and Joshua went, and presented themselves in the tabernacle, their coming, the figure, God himself charging Joshua at the tabernacle, the leadership confirmed by God, the truth that Joshua's commission was sealed by God's own charge at the sanctuary, the figure of the divine confirmation of the leader, the charge from God, the passing of leadership confirmed at the tabernacle, vv.7, the solemn commissioning of Joshua by God); God calling Moses and Joshua to charge Joshua (the commissioning) — the call (to the tabernacle); 'Moses and Joshua went, and presented themselves in the tabernacle of the congregation' (their coming to the sanctuary, the charge from God, the figure, the divine confirmation of the leader, the providence, the call, the LORD appearing in the cloud to follow, vv.15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Behold, thy days approach that thou must die: call Joshua, and present yourselves in the tabernacle of the congregation, that I may give him a charge. And Moses and Joshua went, and presented themselves in the tabernacle of the congregation
God calling Moses and Joshua to the tabernacle to charge Joshua
God telling Moses his death was near and calling him and Joshua to the tabernacle
Joshua's commission sealed by God's own charge at the sanctuary

schools.summary: The LORD appearing in the pillar of cloud at the tabernacle: 'and the LORD appeared in the tabernacle in a pillar of a cloud: and the pillar of the cloud stood over the door of the tabernacle' — God appeared in the pillar of cloud, the token of his presence, at the door of the tabernacle (the and the LORD appeared in the tabernacle in a pillar of a cloud, God's appearance in the cloud, the and the pillar of the cloud stood over the door of the tabernacle, the cloud at the door, the figure, the visible token of God's presence, the pillar of cloud, at the solemn charging of Joshua, the truth that God manifested his presence to seal Joshua's commission, the figure of the glory of God present to confirm his servant, the cloud the sign of his presence, the divine presence at the commissioning, the cloud over the door, the token of God's presence, vv.14); God appearing in the pillar of cloud (the presence) — the appearance (in the cloud); 'the pillar of the cloud stood over the door of the tabernacle' (the cloud at the door, the token of God's presence, the manifestation, the figure, the glory present to confirm the servant, the providence, the appearance, God foretelling the apostasy to follow, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD appeared in the tabernacle in a pillar of a cloud: and the pillar of the cloud stood over the door of the tabernacle
the LORD appearing in the pillar of cloud at the tabernacle

to seal Joshua's commission

schools.summary: That God foretold the people would forsake him and break his covenant: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Behold, thou shalt sleep with thy fathers; and this people will rise up, and go a whoring after the gods of the strangers of the land, whither they go to be among them, and will forsake me, and break my covenant which I have made with them' — the foreknown apostasy — 'behold, thou shalt sleep with thy fathers; and this people will rise up, and go a whoring after the gods of the strangers of the land... and will forsake me, and break my covenant which I have made with them': God foretold that after Moses's death the people would turn to idolatry and forsake him (the witnesses noting the significance — the divine foreknowledge of Israel's apostasy, declared even as the covenant was being confirmed, God knowing beforehand that the people would forsake him and break his covenant, the whoring after other gods foreseen, the lesson — that the heart of man is prone to unfaithfulness, and God knows it from the first, the covenant broken even as it is given, the solemn foretelling of the apostasy, the proneness of the people to idolatry laid bare, the truth that God knows the end from the beginning, the apostasy foreseen and foretold, 'I knew that thou wouldest deal very treacherously,' Isaiah 48:8, the figure — the spiritual adultery of forsaking God for idols, the covenant likened to marriage and idolatry to whoredom, 'they like men have transgressed the covenant,' Hosea 6:7, the truth that the unfaithfulness of God's people is no surprise to him, foreknown and yet not the less their sin, the figure of the broken covenant, the people's treachery foreseen, the faithfulness of God set against the faithlessness of man, the warning that the heart that seems most committed may yet turn away, the certainty that drives the giving of the song as a witness, vv.19, the figure of the divine foreknowledge of human apostasy and the broken covenant); God foretelling, even at Moses's death, that the people would turn to idolatry, the spiritual whoredom foreseen (the foreknown apostasy, Hosea 6:7) — the prophecy (whoring after other gods); 'whither they go to be among them, and will forsake me, and break my covenant which I have made with them' (the forsaking of God and the breaking of his covenant, the unfaithfulness foreknown yet not less their sin, the broken covenant, Isaiah 48:8) — the figure (the spiritual adultery of forsaking God for idols, the providence, the prophecy, God's wrath and hiding of his face to follow, vv.17-18) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does God's foretelling of the apostasy establish?

sides.position: The divine foreknowledge of human apostasy and unfaithfulness - God knowing beforehand, even as the covenant was confirmed, that the people would forsake him and break it (Isaiah 48:8), a figure of the spiritual adultery of forsaking God for idols, the covenant likened to marriage and idolatry to whoredom (Hosea 6:7), the unfaithfulness foreknown yet not the less their sin

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Behold, thou shalt sleep with thy fathers; and this people will rise up, and go a whoring after the gods of the strangers of the land, whither they go to be among them, and will forsake me, and break my covenant which I have made with them
that God foretold the people would forsake him and break his covenant (the crux of the divine foreknowledge of human apostasy)
the divine foreknowledge of Israel's unfaithfulness, declared even as the covenant was confirmed, the whoring after idols and the breaking of the covenant foreseen from the first
a figure of the spiritual adultery of forsaking God for idols, foreknown yet not the less their sin (Isaiah 48:8; Hosea 6:7)

schools.summary: God's anger kindled and his face hidden in the day of apostasy: 'then my anger shall be kindled against them in that day, and I will forsake them, and I will hide my face from them, and they shall be devoured, and many evils and troubles shall befall them; so that they will say in that day, Are not these evils come upon us, because our God is not among us?' — God's anger would be kindled and his face hidden, and the people would own the cause (the then my anger shall be kindled against them in that day, the anger kindled, the and I will forsake them, and I will hide my face from them, God hiding his face, the and they shall be devoured, and many evils and troubles shall befall them, the evils befalling, the so that they will say... because our God is not among us?, the people owning the cause, the figure, the hiding of God's face the worst of judgments, the people devoured by evils, the truth that forsaking God brings his forsaking, his face hidden and evils multiplied, the figure of the withdrawal of God's presence as judgment, 'I hid me, and was wroth,' Isaiah 57:17, the people owning that their troubles came of God's absence, the cause confessed, the judgment of the hidden face, vv.18); God's anger and the hiding of his face, the evils befalling (the judgment, Isaiah 57:17) — the judgment (face hidden); 'Are not these evils come upon us, because our God is not among us?' (the people owning the cause, God's absence, the confession, the figure, the withdrawal of God's presence as judgment, the providence, the judgment, the deeper hiding of God's face to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then my anger shall be kindled against them in that day, and I will forsake them, and I will hide my face from them, and they shall be devoured, and many evils and troubles shall befall them; so that they will say in that day, Are not these evils come upon us, because our God is not among us?
God's anger kindled and his face hidden in the day of apostasy
God's anger kindled and his face hidden for the apostasy, the people devoured by evils
the people owning that their troubles came of God's absence (Isaiah 57:17)

schools.summary: That God would surely hide his face for their turning to other gods: 'and I will surely hide my face in that day for all the evils which they shall have wrought, in that they are turned unto other gods' — God would certainly hide his face because of their idolatry (the and I will surely hide my face in that day, the certain hiding of his face, the for all the evils which they shall have wrought, for their evils, the in that they are turned unto other gods, their idolatry the cause, the figure, the certain withdrawal of God's presence as the just judgment on idolatry, the truth that the hiding of God's face was the sure consequence of turning to other gods, the figure of the lost presence of God, the worst of all judgments, 'thou hidest thy face, they are troubled,' Psalm 104:29, the certain judgment of the hidden face for idolatry, the withdrawal of God for their apostasy, vv.17); God surely hiding his face for their evils (the judgment, Psalm 104:29) — the hiding (of his face); 'in that they are turned unto other gods' (their idolatry the cause of the hidden face, the apostasy, the figure, the lost presence of God for idolatry, the providence, the judgment, the song to be written as a witness to follow, vv.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And I will surely hide my face in that day for all the evils which they shall have wrought, in that they are turned unto other gods
that God would surely hide his face for their turning to other gods
God certainly hiding his face because of their idolatry
the lost presence of God the sure consequence of turning to other gods (Psalm 104:29)

schools.summary: The command to write and teach the song that it might be a witness against the people: 'now therefore write ye this song for you, and teach it the children of Israel: put it in their mouths, that this song may be a witness for me against the children of Israel' — the witness — 'now therefore write ye this song for you, and teach it the children of Israel: put it in their mouths, that this song may be a witness for me against the children of Israel': the song was to be written, taught, and kept in their mouths, to stand as a witness against them when they fell away (the witnesses noting the significance — the word of God set as a permanent witness against the people's foreknown unfaithfulness, the song written, taught, and learned by heart, to testify against them in the day of their apostasy, the lesson — that God's word, when men forsake it, becomes a witness against them, condemning their sin out of their own mouths, the song put in their mouths, so that even in apostasy they would carry the testimony against themselves, the word a witness that leaves them without excuse, the truth that the word preached and known is a savour of life or of death, a witness for or against, 'the word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him in the last day,' John 12:48, the figure — the law and the gospel as a witness against those who reject them, 'this gospel of the kingdom shall be preached... for a witness,' Matthew 24:14, the truth that God so deals with men that his word convicts them, the testimony stored up against the day of judgment, the figure of the song of Moses, taught to the generations, a standing witness to God's faithfulness and the people's treachery, the truth that the means of grace, despised, become the ground of condemnation, the call to write and teach the word, that it abide as a witness, the figure of the word of God set as a standing witness against human unfaithfulness); the song written, taught, and learned by heart, kept in their mouths to be remembered (the word taught and stored, Matthew 24:14) — the command (write and teach); 'that this song may be a witness for me against the children of Israel' (the song standing as a witness against the people in their apostasy, condemning them out of their own mouths, the word that judges, the testimony against them, John 12:48) — the figure (the word a witness against those who reject it, the providence, the witness, God's foreknowledge of their proneness to follow, vv.20-21) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the song written as a witness establish?

sides.position: The word of God set as a standing witness against human unfaithfulness - written, taught, and kept in their mouths to convict them out of their own mouths in the day of apostasy (John 12:48), the word that, despised, becomes a witness against those who reject it (Matthew 24:14), the testimony stored up against the day of judgment

themes: Now therefore write ye this song for you, and teach it the children of Israel: put it in their mouths, that this song may be a witness for me against the children of Israel
the command to write and teach the song that it might be a witness against the people (the crux of the word of God set as a standing witness against human unfaithfulness)
the word of God set as a permanent testimony against the people's foreknown unfaithfulness, written and learned by heart that it might convict them out of their own mouths
the word that, despised, becomes a witness against those who reject it (John 12:48; Matthew 24:14)

schools.summary: That prosperity in the land would lead them to apostasy: 'for when I shall have brought them into the land which I sware unto their fathers, that floweth with milk and honey; and they shall have eaten and filled themselves, and waxen fat; then will they turn unto other gods, and serve them, and provoke me, and break my covenant' — God foretold that fullness and ease in the good land would lead them to forsake him (the for when I shall have brought them into the land which I sware unto their fathers, that floweth with milk and honey, the good land given, the and they shall have eaten and filled themselves, and waxen fat, the fullness and ease, the then will they turn unto other gods, and serve them, and provoke me, and break my covenant, the apostasy, the figure, the danger of prosperity, the full and fat heart turning from God, the truth that ease and plenty would tempt them to forsake God, the fat heart proud, the figure of the peril of abundance, 'Jeshurun waxed fat, and kicked... then he forsook God,' Deuteronomy 32:15, the prosperity that breeds forgetfulness of God, the covenant broken, the warning that the good land's blessings would be the occasion of sin, vv.16); the fullness and ease of the land (the danger, Deuteronomy 32:15) — the fullness (waxen fat); 'then will they turn unto other gods... and break my covenant' (the apostasy bred of prosperity, the covenant broken, the figure, the peril of abundance, the providence, the foretelling, God's knowledge of their imagination to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For when I shall have brought them into the land which I sware unto their fathers, that floweth with milk and honey; and they shall have eaten and filled themselves, and waxen fat; then will they turn unto other gods, and serve them, and provoke me, and break my covenant
that prosperity in the land would lead them to apostasy
God foretelling that fullness and ease in the good land would lead them to forsake him
the prosperity that breeds forgetfulness of God and the breaking of the covenant (Deuteronomy 32:15)

schools.summary: That the song would testify against them, for God knew their imagination: 'and it shall come to pass, when many evils and troubles are befallen them, that this song shall testify against them as a witness; for it shall not be forgotten out of the mouths of their seed: for I know their imagination which they go about, even now, before I have brought them into the land which I sware' — the song would witness against them in their troubles, for God already knew their bent to evil (the and it shall come to pass, when many evils and troubles are befallen them, that this song shall testify against them as a witness, the song testifying, the for it shall not be forgotten out of the mouths of their seed, the song remembered, the for I know their imagination which they go about, even now, before I have brought them into the land, God knowing their bent, the figure, the song an enduring witness, not forgotten, for God knew their proneness to evil, the truth that God knew their imagination even before they entered, the apostasy foreknown, the figure of the searcher of hearts who knows man's bent, 'the LORD knoweth the thoughts of man,' Psalm 94:11, the song that would not be forgotten, the witness abiding, the foreknowledge of God that grounds the witness, vv.19); the song testifying against them, not forgotten (the abiding witness) — the witness (the song testifies); 'I know their imagination which they go about, even now' (God knowing their bent to evil beforehand, the searcher of hearts, Psalm 94:11, the figure, the foreknowledge that grounds the witness, the providence, the foreknowledge, Moses writing the song to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And it shall come to pass, when many evils and troubles are befallen them, that this song shall testify against them as a witness; for it shall not be forgotten out of the mouths of their seed: for I know their imagination which they go about, even now, before I have brought them into the land which I sware
that the song would testify against them, for God knew their imagination
the song an enduring witness not forgotten, for God already knew their bent to evil before they entered the land
the searcher of hearts who knows man's imagination (Psalm 94:11)

schools.summary: Moses writing the song and teaching it to Israel: 'Moses therefore wrote this song the same day, and taught it the children of Israel' — Moses obeyed at once, writing the song and teaching it to the people (the Moses therefore wrote this song the same day, the song written at once, the and taught it the children of Israel, taught to the people, the figure, Moses's prompt obedience, the song written and taught the same day, the truth that Moses obeyed God's command without delay, the song committed and taught, the figure of the faithful servant who delays not to do God's bidding, the word recorded and taught, the song written and learned, the witness prepared, the prompt obedience of Moses, vv.19); Moses writing the song at once (the obedience) — the writing (the same day); 'and taught it the children of Israel' (the song taught to the people, the witness prepared, the figure, the faithful servant who delays not, the providence, the obedience, the charge to Joshua to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Moses therefore wrote this song the same day, and taught it the children of Israel
Moses writing the song and teaching it to Israel
Moses obeying at once, writing the song and teaching it to the people
the witness prepared without delay

schools.summary: God charging Joshua to be strong, for he would bring them in: 'and he gave Joshua the son of Nun a charge, and said, Be strong and of a good courage: for thou shalt bring the children of Israel into the land which I sware unto them: and I will be with thee' — God himself charged Joshua to be strong, with the promise of his presence (the and he gave Joshua the son of Nun a charge, God's charge to Joshua, the and said, Be strong and of a good courage, the courage commanded, the for thou shalt bring the children of Israel into the land which I sware unto them, the task, leading them in, the and I will be with thee, the promise of presence, the figure, God's own charge to Joshua, the courage commanded and the presence promised, the truth that the leader was strengthened by God's charge and the promise 'I will be with thee,' the figure of the divine commissioning, the same promise of presence that grounds all courage, 'I will be with thee,' Joshua 1:5, the task and the promise, Joshua to bring them in, the leadership sealed by God's charge, vv.6-8); God charging Joshua to be strong (the commissioning) — the charge (be strong); 'thou shalt bring the children of Israel into the land... and I will be with thee' (the task and the promise of presence, the leader strengthened, Joshua 1:5, the figure, the divine commissioning with the promise of presence, the providence, the charge, the law put beside the ark to follow, vv.24-26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he gave Joshua the son of Nun a charge, and said, Be strong and of a good courage: for thou shalt bring the children of Israel into the land which I sware unto them: and I will be with thee
God charging Joshua to be strong, for he would bring them in
God himself charging Joshua to be strong, with the task of bringing the people in and the promise 'I will be with thee'
the leadership sealed by God's charge (Joshua 1:5)

schools.summary: Moses finishing the writing of the law in a book: 'and it came to pass, when Moses had made an end of writing the words of this law in a book, until they were finished' — Moses completed the writing of the whole law in a book (the and it came to pass, when Moses had made an end of writing the words of this law in a book, the writing finished, the until they were finished, wholly completed, the figure, the completion of the written law, the whole committed to the book, the truth that the law was wholly written down, the record complete, the figure of the finished Scripture, the word committed entire to writing, the law fully recorded, the deposit complete, the writing of the word finished, vv.9); Moses finishing the writing of the law (the completion) — the writing (finished); 'until they were finished' (the law wholly written, the record complete, the figure, the finished Scripture, the providence, the completion, the law put beside the ark to follow, vv.25-26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And it came to pass, when Moses had made an end of writing the words of this law in a book, until they were finished
Moses finishing the writing of the law in a book
Moses completing the writing of the whole law in a book, the word committed entire to writing
the record complete

schools.summary: Moses commanding the Levites who bore the ark: 'that Moses commanded the Levites, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, saying' — Moses charged the Levites who carried the ark concerning the book of the law (the that Moses commanded the Levites, the command to the Levites, the which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, saying, the bearers of the ark, the figure, the Levites, keepers of the ark, charged with the book of the law, the truth that the written law was committed to those who bore the ark, the keepers of holy things, the figure of the sacred deposit entrusted to the appointed ministers, the law to the Levites, the command that places the law beside the ark, the keeping of the word by the Levites, vv.26); Moses charging the Levites (the command) — the command (to the Levites); 'which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, saying' (the Levites who bore the ark, keepers of holy things, the appointed ministers, the figure, the sacred deposit entrusted to the ministers, the providence, the command, the book put beside the ark to follow, vv.26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: That Moses commanded the Levites, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, saying
Moses commanding the Levites who bore the ark
Moses charging the Levites who carried the ark concerning the book of the law
the sacred deposit entrusted to the keepers of holy things

schools.summary: The command to put the book of the law beside the ark as a witness: 'take this book of the law, and put it in the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, that it may be there for a witness against thee' — the witness — 'take this book of the law, and put it in the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, that it may be there for a witness against thee': the book of the law was to be laid beside the ark, in the most holy place, as a standing witness against the people (the witnesses noting the significance — the written law deposited in the holiest place, beside the ark of the covenant, as a permanent and sacred testimony, the book kept by the ark, the abiding record of the covenant, ready to witness against transgression, the lesson — that the word of God, kept in the sanctuary, stands as a perpetual witness, both to God's covenant and against the people's sin, the law beside the ark, the mercy-seat above and the broken law within, the testimony abiding, the truth that the word is a witness that will rise against the disobedient, 'the word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him,' John 12:48, the figure — the law laid up before God as a testimony, the written word the abiding standard against which the people are judged, 'the testimony of the LORD is sure,' Psalm 19:7, the truth that the Scripture, kept in the church, is both the charter of the covenant and the witness against those who break it, the figure of the word stored in the holiest place, the standing testimony of God, the truth that the same word that is a covenant of life to the obedient is a witness against the disobedient, the call to keep the word in honour as the abiding testimony of God, the figure of the written law set in the holiest place as a standing witness); the book laid beside the ark, in the most holy place, the written law kept in the sanctuary (the sacred deposit, Psalm 19:7) — the command (beside the ark); 'that it may be there for a witness against thee' (the law a standing witness against the people, the abiding testimony of the covenant and against transgression, the word that judges, the testimony, John 12:48) — the figure (the written word the abiding standard against which they are judged, the providence, the witness, Moses calling the elders to hear the song to follow, vv.27-28) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the book of the law set beside the ark establish?

sides.position: The written law set in the holiest place as a standing witness - the abiding record of the covenant and the perpetual testimony against transgression, the word that will judge the disobedient (John 12:48), the sure testimony of the LORD kept before him (Psalm 19:7), both the charter of the covenant and the witness against those who break it

themes: Take this book of the law, and put it in the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, that it may be there for a witness against thee
the command to put the book of the law beside the ark as a witness (the crux of the written law set in the holiest place as a standing testimony)
the written law deposited in the holiest place as a permanent and sacred testimony, the abiding record of the covenant and the witness against transgression
the word that will judge the disobedient, the sure testimony of the LORD (John 12:48; Psalm 19:7)

schools.summary: Moses's knowledge of their rebellion and stiff neck: 'for I know thy rebellion, and thy stiff neck: behold, while I am yet alive with you this day, ye have been rebellious against the LORD; and how much more after my death?' — Moses knew their rebellious and stubborn nature, rebellious even in his lifetime and more so after (the for I know thy rebellion, and thy stiff neck, their rebellion known, the behold, while I am yet alive with you this day, ye have been rebellious against the LORD, rebellious even now, the and how much more after my death?, how much more later, the figure, Moses's knowledge of the people's stubborn rebellion, the ground of the witness, the truth that the people were rebellious even under Moses, and would be worse without him, the figure of the proneness of the heart to rebellion, the stiff neck that resists God, the rebellion that justified the witness against them, 'ye stiffnecked... ye do always resist,' Acts 7:51, the stubbornness foreseen and known, vv.26); the people rebellious even in Moses's lifetime (the stubbornness, Acts 7:51) — the rebellion (known); 'and how much more after my death?' (the rebellion that would grow worse after Moses, the stiff neck, the figure, the proneness of the heart to rebellion, the providence, the rebellion, the gathering of the elders to hear the song to follow, vv.28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For I know thy rebellion, and thy stiff neck: behold, while I am yet alive with you this day, ye have been rebellious against the LORD; and how much more after my death?
Moses's knowledge of their rebellion and stiff neck
Moses knowing the people's rebellious and stubborn nature, rebellious even in his lifetime and worse to come
the ground of the witness against them (Acts 7:51)

schools.summary: Moses gathering the elders to hear the song, with heaven and earth as witnesses: 'gather unto me all the elders of your tribes, and your officers, that I may speak these words in their ears, and call heaven and earth to record against them' — Moses gathered the leaders to hear the song, calling heaven and earth as witnesses (the gather unto me all the elders of your tribes, and your officers, the leaders gathered, the that I may speak these words in their ears, the song spoken to them, the and call heaven and earth to record against them, heaven and earth as witnesses, the figure, the solemn calling of heaven and earth to witness the song against the people, the truth that the song was delivered with the most solemn witnesses, heaven and earth, the figure of the abiding witnesses to the covenant, heaven and earth invoked, 'give ear, O ye heavens... hear, O earth,' Deuteronomy 32:1, the leaders gathered to hear and the witnesses called, the solemn delivery of the song, vv.30); the leaders gathered to hear the song (the assembly) — the gathering (the elders); 'call heaven and earth to record against them' (heaven and earth invoked as witnesses, the solemn testimony, Deuteronomy 32:1, the figure, the abiding witnesses to the covenant, the providence, the witnesses, the song spoken in the ears of all to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Gather unto me all the elders of your tribes, and your officers, that I may speak these words in their ears, and call heaven and earth to record against them
Moses gathering the elders to hear the song, with heaven and earth as witnesses
Moses gathering the leaders to hear the song and calling heaven and earth as the most solemn and abiding witnesses
against the people (Deuteronomy 32:1)

schools.summary: Moses's foreboding that they would corrupt themselves and provoke God after his death: 'for I know that after my death ye will utterly corrupt yourselves, and turn aside from the way which I have commanded you; and evil will befall you in the latter days; because ye will do evil in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger through the work of your hands' — Moses foresaw that after his death they would corrupt themselves, turn aside, and bring evil upon themselves (the for I know that after my death ye will utterly corrupt yourselves, the corruption foreseen, the and turn aside from the way which I have commanded you, the turning aside, the and evil will befall you in the latter days; because ye will do evil in the sight of the LORD, the evil that would follow, the to provoke him to anger through the work of your hands, the idolatry, the figure, Moses's clear foresight of the apostasy, the corruption and the evil to follow, the truth that the people would corrupt themselves and provoke God by idolatry, the evil foreseen, the figure of the prophetic foreknowledge of the apostasy, the latter-day evils foretold, the corruption and turning aside that would bring judgment, vv.16-18, the warning that grounds the song); the corruption and turning aside foreseen (the apostasy) — the corruption (foreseen); 'evil will befall you in the latter days; because ye will do evil... to provoke him to anger' (the evil and provocation that would follow the idolatry, the judgment, the figure, the prophetic foreknowledge of the apostasy, the providence, the foreboding, Moses speaking the song in the ears of all to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For I know that after my death ye will utterly corrupt yourselves, and turn aside from the way which I have commanded you; and evil will befall you in the latter days; because ye will do evil in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger through the work of your hands
Moses's foreboding that they would corrupt themselves and provoke God after his death
Moses foreseeing that after his death they would corrupt themselves, turn aside, and bring evil upon themselves by idolatry
the warning that grounds the song

schools.summary: Moses speaking the song in the ears of all Israel: 'and Moses spake in the ears of all the congregation of Israel the words of this song, until they were ended' — Moses recited the whole song to the assembled people (the and Moses spake in the ears of all the congregation of Israel, spoken to all, the the words of this song, until they were ended, the whole song, the figure, the public delivery of the song to all Israel, the witness proclaimed, the truth that the song was spoken in the hearing of the whole congregation, the testimony given, the figure of the word proclaimed to all the people, the song of Moses that follows in ch.32, the song delivered entire, the witness set before them, the close of the chapter, the song to be sung, ch.32); the song spoken to all the congregation (the proclamation) — the delivery (in their ears); 'the words of this song, until they were ended' (the whole song delivered, the witness proclaimed, the figure, the word proclaimed to all, the providence, the delivery, the song of Moses to follow, ch.32) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Moses spake in the ears of all the congregation of Israel the words of this song, until they were ended
Moses speaking the song in the ears of all Israel
Moses reciting the whole song to the assembled people, the witness proclaimed to all
the close of the chapter, before the song of Moses itself (ch.32)
